Where can we be flexible?

Can we meet halfway?
Victor, good to see you. I will be honest: our price is firm this year. I have very little room to move on the rate.
Understood, and thank you for being direct. Let us start from the common ground: we both want a long-term partnership. So let us see where each of us can be flexible.
Fair enough. What did you have in mind?
Here is a trade-off. If you bring the lead time down to four weeks, we will commit to the full annual volume. That volume is our strongest bargaining chip, and it is real money for you.
Four weeks is tight. I could maybe reach five, but then I would need the two-year contract, not one.
I can live with two years, as long as the service guarantee stays exactly as written. That part is non-negotiable for us, and that is where I have to hold firm.
The guarantee is fine; we never planned to touch it. So our only real sticking point is the rate.
Then let us meet halfway. If you give us a three percent cut, we will sign the two-year deal today, full volume, guarantee included. Do we have an agreement?
If you cut the lead time, we will raise the volume; that volume is our strongest bargaining chip.
The guarantee is non-negotiable, but a longer term is common ground we can build on.
Let us start from the common ground: we both want a long-term partnership.
If you bring the lead time down, we will commit to the full volume.
We cannot sign unless the price drops by five percent.
The service guarantee is non-negotiable, and that is where I have to hold firm.
Let us meet halfway: if you give us three percent, we will sign today.

Okay, let me think this through before I walk in. My target is a five percent price cut and a four-week lead time. My walk-away point is clear: if the lead time stays above six weeks, there is no deal, because our branches cannot wait that long. My strongest bargaining chip is volume. We are ready to commit to the full annual order, and that is worth real money to them. So the trade is simple. If they bring the lead time down to four weeks, we will lock in the volume for the whole year. The price is where I can be flexible, but only in exchange for something. I will not give a single concession for free. The one thing that is non-negotiable is the service guarantee. On that, I hold firm. Everything else, we can trade. If we hit a deadlock on price, I change the variable. I do not chase it.

Hi Victor, it is Marina. Before your supplier meeting tomorrow, here are three quick reminders. First, open by mapping the common ground. Name the things you both already agree on, so the room starts from yes, not from no. Second, and this is the big one, never give a concession without getting one back. Do not gift, trade. If you move on the contract length, you get movement on the price. Every give needs a take. Third, know your one non-negotiable and say it calmly. You do not need to raise your voice to hold firm; a quiet, steady no is stronger than an angry one. And if you reach a deadlock, do not keep pushing the same point. Change a variable instead. Offer a longer term, a faster payment, a bigger volume, anything that lets both sides move without losing face. Go in calm, trade well, and protect that one line you will not cross. You have got this.
